title: Syllabus
text: This 2-credit course introduces basic concepts and methods for statistical learning, with an emphasis on modern health science applications. Topics include linear regression with regularization, supervised classification, unsupervised clustering, and dimensionality reduction. The target audience is second-year Biostatistics master’s students or early PhD students with interests in statistical learning techniques for health science data. Students will be trained with hands-on materials to understand the methods, implement the algorithms, and interpret results in real applications.

title: Syllabus
text: This 2-credit course introduces basic concepts and methods for statistical learning, with an emphasis on modern health science applications. Topics include linear regression with regularization, supervised classification, unsupervised clustering, and dimensionality reduction. The target audience is second-year Biostatistics master’s students or early PhD students with interests in statistical learning techniques for health science data. Students will be trained with hands-on materials to understand the methods, implement the algorithms, and interpret results in real applications.

title: Syllabus
text:
Prerequisites
BIOST 2141, 2142, and 3011. Students are expected to have programming experience in R, or in a low-level language such as C, C++, Java, or Fortran. Prerequisites can be met with other relevant coursework or experience with consent from the instructor.

title: Syllabus
text:
Learning objectives
By the end of this course, students will be able to:

Explain the motivation and intuition behind the statistical learning methods covered in class.
Demonstrate basic theory proofs for selected fundamental concepts and methods.
Implement methods on real datasets in health sciences.
Produce an analysis pipeline and interpret the results in a real application.

title: Syllabus
text:
Textbook
Primary textbook (highly recommended; free PDF):

James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani. An Introduction to Statistical Learning: with Applications in R. Springer, 2013. https://www.statlearning.com/

Optional references:

Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman. The Elements of Statistical Learning. Springer, 2009.
Prince. Understanding Deep Learning. MIT Press, 2024.
Bishop. Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning. Springer, 2006.

title: Syllabus
text:
Computing
We will use R as our main programming language. RStudio with R Markdown is recommended for homework. Templates are provided.

title: Syllabus
text:
Grading



Component
Weight




Homework 1
25%


Homework 2
25%


Homework 3
25%


Final project presentation
25%



Letter-grade cut-offs: A 90–100%, B 80–89%, C 70–79%, D 60–69%, F < 60%. Plus/minus assigned by thirds within each band.

Homework policy
Three homework assignments — a mix of theory, simulation, and applied data analysis in R. Submissions are electronic via Canvas. Discussion of homework with classmates is allowed, but each student must independently write and implement their own solution. Solutions are graded on both correctness and clarity. Partial credit is given for identifying gaps in an argument when a complete solution cannot be derived.
Late homework: 80% credit if 0–1 days late, 50% credit if 1–2 days, 0% if more than 2 days. Exceptions for sickness or emergency require the instructor be informed in advance.


Final project
By 10/03, students submit a one-page proposal identifying either a dataset to analyze or a methodological paper to review. On 12/05, each student gives a 5-minute presentation summarizing their work. See the project page for details.

title: Syllabus
text:
Academic integrity
Cheating and plagiarism are not allowed and will be reported. See the University of Pittsburgh’s Policy on Academic Integrity.

title: Syllabus
text:
Disability services
If you have a disability for which you are or may be requesting accommodation, please contact both the instructor and Disability Resources and Services (DRS) — 140 William Pitt Union, (412) 648-7890, drsrecep@pitt.edu, (412) 228-5347 for P3 ASL users — as early as possible in the term.

title: Syllabus
text:
Title IX & sexual misconduct
University faculty are required to report instances of sexual misconduct, including harassment and sexual violence, to the Office of Civil Rights and Title IX. To make a confidential report, the University Counseling Center is available at 412-648-7930 (8:30 AM – 5 PM M–F) and 412-648-7856 (after hours). Pittsburgh Action Against Rape: 1-866-363-7273 (24/7). For an immediate safety concern, contact the University of Pittsburgh Police: 412-624-2121.
An exception to the reporting requirement applies to disclosures shared as a relevant part of an academic project, classroom discussion, or course assignment.

title: Final Project
text: The final project is 25% of the course grade. Each student presents individual work in a 5-minute talk on the last day of class (12/05). You may choose either an applied analysis of a real dataset or a review of a methodological paper.

title: Final Project
text:
Key dates

10/03 — One-page proposal due (dataset or paper, plus planned scope)
12/05 — In-class presentations (5 min/student)

title: Final Project
text:
Project description
📄 Full project description (PDF) · 📝 Source (R Markdown)

title: Final Project
text:
Tips on choosing a project

For applied projects: pick a dataset where at least two methods from the course are plausibly relevant — that gives you something to compare in your talk. Public health data, genomic / single-cell, EHR-derived, or NHANES-style cohorts all work well.
For methodology reviews: pick a paper recent enough to have follow-up work (so you have something to contextualize) but old enough to have settled into the literature. Aim for one core paper and 2–3 related citations.
Scope honestly: 5 minutes is short. Plan your talk as ~3 main slides plus framing — that’s the unit of analysis to plan around, not the project as a whole.

title: Decision trees
text:
Recursive binary splitting; impurity measures (Gini, entropy); tree pruning via cost-complexity; trees as interpretable but high-variance learners.
Assigned reading: ISLR §8.1

title: Decision trees
text:
Materials
Slides: Slides | Annotated slides
Code: app1.R class10_lab1.Rmd fig1.R fig2.R fig3.R fig4.R
Supplementary readings: Breiman (2001) Chen and Ishwaran (2012) Watanabe et al. (2021)

title: Clustering II
text:
Gaussian mixture models as soft clustering; the EM algorithm; model selection via BIC; comparing GMMs to k-means.
Assigned reading: PRML §9.1–9.2

title: Clustering II
text:
Materials
Slides: Slides | Annotated slides

title: Classification II
text:
Maximum margin classifiers; the soft-margin extension; kernel SVMs for non-linear decision boundaries.
Assigned reading: ISLR ch. 9

title: Classification II
text:
Materials
Slides: Slides | Annotated slides
Code: app1.R fig1.R fig2.R fig3.R lab1.R lab1.Rmd

title: Regression II
text:
Polynomials, step functions, regression splines, smoothing splines, and an introduction to the kernel trick — moving beyond the linear model while keeping the regression framework.
Assigned reading: ISLR ch. 7

title: Regression II
text:
Materials
Slides: Slides | Annotated slides | Split view
Code: fig1.R fig2.R fig3.R fig4.R fig5.R helper.R lab1.R lab2.R
Supplementary readings: Stricker et al. (2017) Zimbalist et al. (2025)

title: Training statistical learning models
text:
Under- and over-fitting; the training/validation/test split; k-fold cross-validation and the bootstrap. Foundations for every method we’ll see later in the course.
Assigned reading: ISLR §2.2, ch. 5

title: BIOST 2155
text: A graduate-level introduction to statistical learning methods, with applications drawn from modern health science data. Topics include linear regression with regularization, classification, clustering, dimensionality reduction, and ensemble methods.

title: Homework 3
text:
Instructions
Open the R Markdown template, knit it once to confirm your environment is set up, and complete each problem in the appropriate section. Submit a knitted PDF (and optionally the .Rmd source) via Canvas before the deadline.






TipReproducibility



Set a random seed at the top of your .Rmd so your simulation results don’t change between knits. Use set.seed(2155) (or any fixed integer).

title: Assignments

Submission and grading

Submissions go through Canvas as a knitted PDF (and optionally the .Rmd source).
Solutions are graded on both correctness and clarity — use whitespace, write clear comments, and label your output.
Partial credit is given for identifying gaps in an argument when a complete solution cannot be derived.

title: Assignments

Collaboration
Discussion among students is allowed and encouraged. Each student must independently write and implement their own solution. Cite any external sources you consulted.

title: Introduction
text:
Course overview and the landscape of statistical learning. We distinguish supervised problems (regression, classification) from unsupervised ones (clustering, dimensionality reduction), and motivate the rest of the semester through health-science examples.
Assigned reading: ISLR ch. 2

title: Introduction
text:
Materials
Slides: Slides | Split view
Code: fig1.R fig2.R fig3.R fig4.R
Supplementary readings: islr_class1 reading1_breimann2001 reading2_gelman2020
Extras: Extra_NoisyLabels.pdf

title: Regression I
text:
Linear regression as a baseline; best-subset and stepwise selection; ridge regression and the lasso as principled alternatives when p is large or predictors are correlated.
Assigned reading: ISLR ch. 3, 6

title: Regression I
text:
Materials
Slides: Slides | Annotated slides | Split view
Code: fig1.R fig2.R fig3.R fig4.R helper.R model1.R
Supplementary readings: Frank and Friedman (1993) Heinze et al. (2017)

title: Classification I
text:
Logistic regression as a discriminative classifier; LDA, QDA, and naive Bayes as generative classifiers. Decision boundaries and what each approach assumes about the data.
Assigned reading: ISLR ch. 4

title: Classification I
text:
Materials
Slides: Slides | Annotated slides
Code: extra_simulation.R fig1.R fig2.R fig3.R fig4.R fig5.R lab1.Rmd
Supplementary readings: Van Calster et al. (2025)
Extras: classification_recap.pdf

title: Clustering I
text:
k-means as an EM-style algorithm; choosing k; silhouette scores and gap statistics; what cluster evaluation actually tells you.
Assigned reading: ISLR §10.3

title: Clustering I
text:
Materials
Slides: Slides
Code: fig1.R fig2.R lab1.Rmd
Supplementary readings: Moraru et al. (2025) Prabakaran et al. (2019)

title: Dimensionality reduction
text:
PCA as variance maximization and as a reconstruction problem; the SVD; choosing the number of components; PCR as a regression follow-up.
Assigned reading: ISLR §6.3

title: Dimensionality reduction
text:
Materials
Slides: Slides | Annotated slides

title: Ensemble learning
text:
Why averaging helps; bagging and the bootstrap; random forests and feature subsampling; boosting (AdaBoost, gradient boosting) as a different ensemble philosophy.
Assigned reading: ISLR §8.2
